content.full_text:
, ' 
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
December 13, 2016 
Alan Seem 
Shearman & Sterling LLP 
1460 El Camino Real, 2nd Floor 
Menlo Park, CA 94025-4110 
Re: 
Your Request for lnfonnal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-16-236 
Dear Mr. Seem: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political 
Reform Act ( the "Act")1 on behalf of the China Silicon Valley Business Development (''CSV"), a 
California non-profit corporation, and certain unnamed mayors and council members from Silicon 
Valley. 
The Act imposes duties on public o_fficials and on donors to the o_fficials.2 Because your 
questions are general in nature and you have not named officials on whose behalf you request 
advice, we arc treating your request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance docs not 
provide the rcqucstor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal written advice. (Section 
83114; Re!:,rulation 18329(c)(3).) 
Nothing in this letter should be construed to apply to past conduct. (Regulation 
18329(b)(8)(A) and (c)( 4)(A).) Although this letter regards a trip which has already occurred, this 
letter addresses future reporting obligations arising out of the trip. Further, because the Fair 
Political Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering 
advice, this letter is based on the facts presented. (/11 re Ogll!sby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
FACTS 
You are writing to request advice related to the China Silicon Valley 2016 Autumn China 
Trip ("Autumn China Trip'') organized by CSV. You state that CSV has organized the trip for 
Silicon Valley mayors and council members to travel to China. You provided a copy of the 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Go\·emment Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Cahfomia Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Di\ision 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 
l Section 89521 provides: "Any person who makes or receives an honorarium, gift, or expenditure in violation 
of this chapter is liable in a ci\1il action brought by the Commission for an amount of up to three times the amount of the 
unlawful honorarium, gift. or expenditure. 


File No. 1-16-236 
Page No. 2 
invitation to the "2016 Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei Venture Capital Summit" with the following 
itinerary: 
November 14, 2016 - Arrival in China. Dinner 
November 14 - 16, 2016 - Celebration of the Silicon Valley Enterprises arrival, 
and 2016 Beijing-Tianjin-Hebci Venture Capital Summit 
November 17-18, 2016 - Beijing Global A J Summit celebration of A I Program 
November 18, 2016- Return 
The purpose of the trip is to improve business cooperation between China and the Silicon 
Valley, help create jobs and increase economic activity in both China and the Silicon Valley, 
continue to facilitate investment and international trade, and promote communications between 
China and the Silicon Valley region. While in China, the public officials will have opportunities to 
participate in meetings with local government officials, potential investors, and CEOs from local 
high tech companies. This trip is similar in purpose to previous CSV organized Silicon Valley 
Mayors' China trips conducted in 2014, 2015, and spring of 2016. We have previously issued 
letters regarding these trips.3 
You state with respect to financing that CSV will not provide any funds or services with 
monetary value to the public officials in connection with the 2016 Autumn China Trip. The Gu'an 
Emerging Industry Demonstration Zone of Hebei Province, a local Chinese government authority, 
will pay for the following estimated expenses for the public officials attending the event: 
• All international air travel expenses to and from ChinaǗ S2,000 per person 
• Remaining expenses for travel, lodging and meals within Chinaǘ S3,000 per person 
The CSV web site notes that Silicon Valley business leaders are also invited to attend, and 
to sponsor this trip at levels from S20,000 down to S2T500 (not traveling to China). Business leaders 
that sponsor the trip arc offered media coverage both in China and Silicon Valley. connections with 
potential Chinese investors and business partners, and marketing opportunities in China. ln a 
subsequent email, your assistant, Mr. Chen Ye, explained that the money sponsored by local 
businesses goes to CS V's Bank of America account and that none of this sponsorship money was 
used to pay for the officials' expenses in the China trips. The officials' expenses were covered 
either by Chinese government agencies or by 50l(c)(3) organizations for all of CS V's China trips. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's Gift limit Pmvisious: 
A gift is defined under Section 82028(a) as: 
3 Informational Assistance Letters, Seem No. 1-16-079 and No. 1-15-189. and collecti,·e requests for advice 
Reyes. et al Nos A· 14-071, A-14-072, A-14-078 and A-14-079. 


·' 
File No. 1-16-236 
Page No. 3 
... any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that 
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or 
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in 
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official 
status. 
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of 
gifts by local public officials in three ways: 
• 
Gift Limit of $460: The Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public 
officials. The current limit is $460 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; 
Regulation 18940.2.) This gift limit applies to all public officials. (Sections 89503(a) and 
87200.) 
• Gift Reporting: So that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the 
Act imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gift (or any 
gifts that aggregate to S50 or more from the same source) received during the calendar year 
arc disclosed on the officials' statements of economic interests. (Sections 87200 - 87210.) 
• Conflict of Interest Disqualification: The Act prohibits any public official from making. 
participating in making, or using his or her position to influence the outcome of a 
governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $460 
or more provided to, received by, or promised to the official within the 12 months prior to 
the date the decision is made. (Sections 87100 & 87103(e); Regulations 18700 & 18703.4.) 
Under some circumstances, even though a payment for transportation, lodging. and 
subsistence may be a reportable gift under the Act, the gift is not subject to the Act's current $460 
gift limit. In this regard, Section 89506(a) provides (in pertinent part) an exception from the gift 
limits for certain travel payments: 
Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual 
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably related to a 
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national. or 
international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of 
the following apply: 
( 1) .... 
(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a 
foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private 
educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation 
Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section 
501 ( c )(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person domiciled outside the 
United States who substantially satisfies the requirements for tax - exempt status 
under Section 501 (c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. 


File No. I-I 6-236 
Page No. 4 
According to the facts, the trip's purpose is to improve business cooperation between China 
and the Silicon Valley, to help create jobs and increase economic activity in both China and the 
Silicon Valley, to facilitate investment and international trade, and to promote communications 
between China and the Silicon Valley region. While in China, the public officials will have 
opportunities to participate in meetings with local government officials, potential investors, and 
CEOs from local high-tech companies. Based on these facts, the travel is directly related to a 
legislative or governmental purpose. 
You state that Gu'an Emerging Industry Demonstration Zone of Hebei Province, a local 
Chinese government authority, will pay for all international air travel expenses to and from China, 
as well as remaining expenses for travel, lodging and meals within China during the 2016 Autumn 
China Trip. Therefore, the travel is provided by a foreign government and meets the requirements 
of Section 89506(a)(2)." 
Consequently, the specified payments arc reportable gifts that are 1101 s11bjccl to g({t limits 
pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2). We discuss the conflict of interest issues further below. 
Please note that any payments made for items other than travel and related lodging and 
subsistence, such as payments for entertainment purposes, may be considered a reportable gift, 
subject to the S460 gift limit, and subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act. For all 
gifts, if the official reimburses the donor or donor's intcnncdiary for the gift within 30 days, no gift 
is considered to have been received or accepted. (Regulation 1894l(c)(3).) 
C011flicl of/11/crcst Disqual(fication 
As mentioned above, a public official who receives gift(s) of $460 or more may have a 
financial conflict of interest under the Act. (Sections 87 IOO ct seq.) The official must disqualify 
himself or herself from voting or otherwise participating in a governmental decision affecting that 
source, if the payment was received or promised to the official within '2 months preceding the 
decision. Therefore, if the public officials accept gifts \'alued at $460 or more, they may be 
prohibited from participating in governmental decisions affecting the source of those gifts. 
Nonpro.firs rhat Regularĸ\' Hos/ Trm·cl.for Elected Officials 
Finally, please be aware that Section 89506(0, a recent statute effective January 1, 2016, 
provides that certain nonprofits that regularly organize and host travel for elected officials must 
disclose certain donors. A 501 (c)(3) or 501 (c)(4) nonprofit that { 1) "regularly organizes and hosts 
travel for elected officials," (2) "makes payments, advances, or reimbursements that total more than 
ten thousand dollars ($10,000) in a calendar year, or that total more than ti ve thousand dollars 
4 Note that Regulation l 8945(a) pro\'ides: "1 he person who makes the gift to the official(s) is the source of the 
gift unless that person is acting as an intermediary. The person is acting as an intennediary for the source of the gift 
when the gift to the official was pro,ided under any of the following conditions: (I) the person recei\'es a payment from 
a source and the payment is made to the official after the source identifies the official as the imended recipient of the 
gift: (2) the person receives a payment from a source after soliciting the payment with the understanding that the 
payment \\ ill be used for the sole or primary purpose of making a gift to an official; or (3) the person receives a 
payment from a source after the payment was solicited by the official or the official's agent for the purpose of making a 
gift to the official." 


. 
r 
File No. I-16-236 
Page No. 5 
($5,000) in a calendar year for a single person, for travel by an elected state officer or local elected 
officeholder," must disclose the names of donors who did both of the following in the preceding 
year: "(A) Donated one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more to the nonprofit organization" and "{B) 
Accompanied an elected state officer or local elected officeholder, either personally or through an 
agent, employee, or representative, for any portion of the travel [covered by the travel payments, 
advances, or reimbursements under 89506(a))." 
Your facts indicate that CSV, a nonprofit organization, is not financing the public officials' 
travel expenses. However, if you believe the above provision applies, contact us for further advice. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division
